title: Tangents to polar curves
description: Practice tangent lines of functions defined with polar coordinates.
topic_path: Parametric equations, polar coordinates, and vector-valued functions
Defining polar coordinates and differentiating in polar form

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the polar curve $r=4\sin(3\theta)$.

**What is the slope of the tangent line to the curve $r$ when $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{3}$?**  
*Give an exact expression.*

$\text{slope }=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
The slope of the tangent line at a point is equal to $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at that point. In the case of polar curves, we can use the relationship:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\left( \dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right)}$

For a polar curve, $x=\goldE{r}\cos(\theta)$ and $y=\goldE{r}\sin(\theta)$. Therefore, in our problem we have:

$\begin{align}
x&=\goldE{4\sin(3\theta)}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
y&=\goldE{4\sin(3\theta)} \sin(\theta)
\end{align}$

**The slope of the tangent line to the curve $r$ when $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{3}$ equals $\sqrt{3}$.**

The graph of the tangent is shown.

![A polar curve and a tangent line are graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 6 in increments of 1. The circumference units go from 0 to eleven sixths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one sixth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve is a rose curve which has three petal-like loops extending at regular intervals from the origin. Moving counterclockwise from the right, the loops move out to the following points before looping back to the origin. (4, one sixth pi), (4, five sixths pi), and (4, three halves pi). A tangent line moves diagonally upward, intersecting the curve at the origin. All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f49cde441738a3ab44397c084c8b5cfcae4f2023)

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the polar curve $r=\tan(\theta)$.

**What is the slope of the tangent line to the curve $r$ when $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$ ?**  
*Give an exact expression.*

$\text{slope }=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
The slope of the tangent line at a point is equal to $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at that point. In the case of polar curves, we can use the relationship:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\left( \dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right)}$

For a polar curve, $x=\goldE{r}\cos(\theta)$ and $y=\goldE{r}\sin(\theta)$. Therefore, in our problem we have:

$\begin{align}
x&=\goldE{\tan(\theta)}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
y&=\goldE{\tan(\theta)} \sin(\theta)
\end{align}$

**The slope of the tangent line to the curve $r$ when $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ equals $3$.**

The graph of the tangent is shown.

![A polar curve is graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 3 in increments of 1. The circumference units go from 0 to seven fourths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one fourth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve is symmetric about the origin, where the shape of the curve on each side of the origin is a parabola. The top curve is upward facing and the bottom is downward facing. The top curve moves downward through points (1, three fourths pi) to (0, 0), then moves upward through (1, one fourth pi). The bottom curve moves upward through (1, five fourths pi) to (0, 0), then moves downward through (1, seven fourths pi). A tangent line moves diagonally upward, intersecting the top curve at (1, one fourth pi). All values estimated.
](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/859e5829c2e362d9f3ee8f97bee200c3.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the polar curve $r=2+6\sin(\theta)$.

**What is the slope of the tangent line to the curve $r$ when $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$?**  
*Give an exact expression.*

$\text{slope }=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
The slope of the tangent line at a point is equal to $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at that point. In the case of polar curves, we can use the relationship:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\left( \dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right)}$

For a polar curve, $x=\goldE{r}\cos(\theta)$ and $y=\goldE{r}\sin(\theta)$. Therefore, in our problem we have:

$\begin{align}
x&=\goldE{(2+6\sin(\theta))}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
y&=\goldE{(2+6\sin(\theta))} \sin(\theta)
\end{align}$

**The slope of the tangent line to the curve $r$ when $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{6}$ equals $2\sqrt{3}$.**

The graph of the tangent is shown.

![A polar curve and a tangent line are graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 8 in increments of 1. The circumference units go from 0 to eleven sixths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one sixth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve is a concentric loop that starts at the origin. The outer loop moves clockwise through points (2, pi), (5, five sixths pi), (7, two thirds pi), (8, one half pi), (7, one third pi), (5, one sixth pi), (2, 0), (0, 0). The inner loop then moves through points (1, five sixths pi), (3, two thirds pi), (4, one half pi), (3, one third pi), (1, one sixth pi) and (0, 0). A tangent line moves diagonally upward, intersecting the curve at (5, one sixth pi). All values estimated.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/aba97b784dad5109edd1d7f9fee170df.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the polar curve $r=3\sin(\theta)$.

**What is the slope of the tangent line to the curve $r$ when $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{3}$?**  
*Give an exact expression.*

$\text{slope }=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
The slope of the tangent line at a point is equal to $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at that point. In the case of polar curves, we can use the relationship:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\left( \dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right)}$

For a polar curve, $x=\goldE{r}\cos(\theta)$ and $y=\goldE{r}\sin(\theta)$. Therefore, in our problem we have:

$\begin{align}
x&=\goldE{3\sin(\theta)}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
y&=\goldE{3\sin(\theta)} \sin(\theta)
\\\\
&=3\sin^2(\theta)
\end{align}$

Finally, we evaluate $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at $\blueD{\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{3}}$.

$\begin{align}
\left.  \dfrac{dy}{dx} \right| _{\theta =\tfrac{\pi }{3}}&=\tan\left(2\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}\right)\right)
\\\\&=\tan\left(\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)
\\\\
&=-\sqrt{3}
\end{align}$

**The slope of the tangent line to the curve $r$ when $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{3}$ equals $-\sqrt{3}$.**

The graph of the tangent is shown.

![A polar curve and a tangent line are graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 6 in increments of 1. The circumference units go from 0 to eleven sixths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one sixth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve is a circle with a radius of 1.5 centered on (1.5, one half pi). It passes through the following points. (0, 0), (1, five sixths pi), (2.5, two thirds pi), (3, one half pi), (2.5, one third pi), and (1, one sixth pi). A tangent line moves diagonally downward, intersecting the circle at (3, one third pi). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e48d15c72fe728efb84194c781ce903501360097)

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the polar curve $r=\cos(\theta)$.

**What is the slope of the tangent line to the curve $r$ when $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$?**  
*Give an exact expression.*

$\text{slope }=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
The slope of the tangent line at a point is equal to $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at that point. In the case of polar curves, we can use the relationship:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\left( \dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right)}$

For a polar curve, $x=\goldE{r}\cos(\theta)$ and $y=\goldE{r}\sin(\theta)$. Therefore, in our problem we have:

$\begin{align}
x&=\goldE{\cos(\theta)}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
&=\cos^2(\theta)
\\\\
y&=\goldE{\cos(\theta)} \sin(\theta)
\\\\
&=0.5\sin(2\theta)
\end{align}$

**The slope of the tangent line to the curve $r$ when $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ equals $0$.**

The graph of the tangent is shown.

![A polar curve and a tangent line are graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 2 in increments of 0.5. The circumference units go from 0 to fifteen eighths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one eighth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve is a circle with a radius of 0.5 centered on (0.5, 0). It passes through the following points. (1, 0), (0.75, seven fourths pi), (0, 0), and (0.75, one fourth pi). A horizontal tangent line intersects the circle at (0.75, one fourth pi). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/abffd2b068ac63b1c396a5f8eab76a66aefcc04e)

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the polar curve $r=\theta ^{2}$.

**What is the slope of the tangent line to the curve $r$ when $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$?**  
*Give an exact expression.*

$\text{slope }=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
The slope of the tangent line at a point is equal to $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at that point. In the case of polar curves, we can use the relationship:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\left( \dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right)}$

For a polar curve, $x=\goldE{r}\cos(\theta)$ and $y=\goldE{r}\sin(\theta)$. Therefore, in our problem we have:

$\begin{align}
x&=\goldE{\theta^{2}}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
y&=\goldE{\theta^{2}} \sin(\theta)
\end{align}$

**The slope of the tangent line to the curve $r$ when $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ equals $-\dfrac{4}{\pi}$.**

The graph of the tangent is shown.

![A polar curve and a tangent line are graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 8 in increments of 1. The circumference units go from 0 to eleven sixths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one sixth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve moves upward right with increasing steepness from (0, 0) to (2.5, one half pi), then continues downward left through (4.5, two thirds pi) and (7, five sixths pi). A tangent line moves diagonally downward, intersecting the curve at (2.5, one half pi). All values estimated.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/68614b26322a9de95cc17879fb0fd435.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the polar curve $r=4\sin(5\theta)$.

**What is the equation of the tangent line to the curve $r$ at $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{3}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The slope of the tangent line at a point is equal to $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at that point. In the case of polar curves, we can use the relationship:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\left( \dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right)}$

Then we can use the [[☃ definition 1]] to complete the equation for the tangent line through $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{3}$.

For a polar curve, $x=\goldE{r}\cos(\theta)$ and $y=\goldE{r}\sin(\theta)$. Therefore, in our problem we have:

$\begin{align}
x&=\goldE{4\sin(5\theta)}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
y&=\goldE{4\sin(5\theta)} \sin(\theta)
\end{align}$

We now find $x$ and $y$ at the point $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{3}$.

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{x\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)}&=4\sin\left(\displaystyle\dfrac{5\pi}{3}\right)\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)
\\\\
&=4\left(-\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)
\\\\
&=\maroonD{-\sqrt{3}}
\\\\
\\\\
\purpleD{y\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)}&=4\sin\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{3}\right)\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)
\\\\
&=4\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}
\right)
\\\\
&=\purpleD{-3}
\end{align}$

**Therefore the equation of our tangent line is :**

$\begin{align}
y-(\purpleD{-3})&=\greenD{\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}(x-(\maroonD{\sqrt{3}}))
\\\\
y+3&=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(x+\sqrt{3})
\end{align}$

The graph of the tangent is shown.

![A polar curve and a tangent line are graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 4 in increments of 1. The circumference units go from 0 to eleven sixths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one sixth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve is a rose curve which has five petal-like loops extending at regular intervals from the origin. Moving counterclockwise from the top, the loops move out to the following points before looping back to the origin. (4, one half pi), (4, eleven twelfths pi), (4, thirty one twenty fourths pi), (4, forty one twenty fourths pi), and (4, one twelfth pi). A tangent line moves diagonally upward, intersecting the curve on the third petal at (3.5, four thirds pi). All values estimated.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2d68eb30213adc4bac7e83437bd8699d.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the polar curve $r=\sin(\theta)\cos(\theta)$.

**What is the equation of the tangent line to the curve $r$ at $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{3}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The slope of the tangent line at a point is equal to $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at that point. In the case of polar curves, we can use the relationship:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\left( \dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right)}$

Then we can use the [[☃ definition 1]] to complete the equation for the tangent line through $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{3}$.

For a polar curve, $x=\goldE{r}\cos(\theta)$ and $y=\goldE{r}\sin(\theta)$. Therefore, in our problem we have:

$\begin{align}
x&=\goldE{\sin(\theta)\cos(\theta)}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
&=\sin(\theta)\cos^2(\theta)
\\\\
y&=\goldE{\sin(\theta)\cos(\theta)} \sin(\theta)
\\\\
&=\sin^2(\theta)\cos(\theta)
\end{align}$

We now find $x$ and $y$ at the point $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{3}$.

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{x\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}\right)}&=\sin\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}\right)\cos^2\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}\right)
\\\\
&=\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2
\\\\
&=\maroonD{\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{8}}
\\\\
\\\\
\purpleD{y\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}\right)}&=\sin^2\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}\right)\cos\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}\right)
\\\\
&=\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2\left(\dfrac{{1}}{2}\right)
\\\\
&=\purpleD{\dfrac{{3}}{8}}
\end{align}$

**Therefore the equation of our tangent line is:**

 $\begin{align}
y-\purpleD{\dfrac{3}{8}}&=\greenD{\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{5}}\left(x-\maroonD{\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{8}}\right)
\end{align}$

The graph of the tangent is shown.

![A polar curve and a tangent line are graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 1.2 in increments of 0.4. The circumference units go from 0 to eleven sixths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one sixth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve is a rose curve which has four petal-like loops extending at regular intervals from the origin. Moving counterclockwise from the top right, the loops move out to the following points before looping back to the origin. (0.5, one fourth pi), (0.5, three fourths pi), (0.5, five fourths pi), and (0.5, seven fourths pi). A tangent line moves diagonally upward, intersecting the curve on the first petal at (0.4, one third pi). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cbcd1f82ef5138f914e1ae795897dd287f1992c1)

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the polar curve $r=\sin^2\left(2\theta\right) $.

**What is the equation of the tangent line to the curve $r$ at $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{4}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The slope of the tangent line at a point is equal to $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at that point. In the case of polar curves, we can use the relationship:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\left( \dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right)}$

Then we can use the [[☃ definition 1]] to complete the equation for the tangent line through $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{4}$.

For a polar curve, $x=\goldE{r}\cos(\theta)$ and $y=\goldE{r}\sin(\theta)$. Therefore, in our problem we have:

$\begin{align}
x&=\goldE{\sin^2(2\theta)}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
y&=\goldE{\sin^2(2\theta)} \sin(\theta)
\end{align}$

We now find $x$ and $y$ at the point $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{4}$.

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{x\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right)}&=\sin^2\left(2\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right)\right)\cos\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right)
\\\\
&=\sin^2\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)
\\\\
&=\maroonD{\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}}
\\\\
\\\\
\purpleD{y\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right)}&=\sin^2\left(2\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right)\right)\sin\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right)
\\\\
&=\sin^2\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)
\\\\
&=\purpleD{\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}}
\end{align}$

**Therefore the equation of our tangent line is: **

$\begin{align}
y-\purpleD{\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}}&=\greenD{(-1)}\left(x-\maroonD{\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}}\right)
\\\\
y-\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}&=-\left(x-\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)
\end{align}$

The graph of the tangent is shown.

![A polar curve and a tangent line are graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 1 in increments of 1. The circumference units go from 0 to fifteen eighths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one eighth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve is a rose curve which has four petal-like loops extending at regular intervals from the origin. Moving counterclockwise from the top right, the loops move out to the following points before looping back to the origin. (1, one fourth pi), (1, three fourths pi), (1, five fourths pi), (1, seven fourths pi). A tangent line moves diagonally downward, intersecting the curve on the first petal at (1, one fourth pi).](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b80798db1f92df63448a8655f7fb15ec.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the polar curve $r=4\sin(\theta)$.

**What is the equation of the tangent line to the curve $r$ at $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{6}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The slope of the tangent line at a point is equal to $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at that point. In the case of polar curves, we can use the relationship:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\left( \dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right)}$

Then we can use the [[☃ definition 1]] to complete the equation for the tangent line through $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{6}$.

For a polar curve, $x=\goldE{r}\cos(\theta)$ and $y=\goldE{r}\sin(\theta)$. Therefore, in our problem we have:

$\begin{align}
x&=\goldE{4\sin(\theta)}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
&=2\sin(2\theta)
\\\\
y&=\goldE{4\sin(\theta)} \sin(\theta)
\\\\
&=4\sin^2(\theta)
\end{align}$

Evaluating $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at $\blueD{\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{6}}$ gives us the slope of our tangent line.

$\begin{align}
\greenD{\left. \dfrac{dy}{dx}\right| _{\theta=\tfrac{\pi}{6}}}&=\tan\left(2\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}\right)\right) 
\\\\
&=\tan\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)
\\\\
&=\greenD{\sqrt{3}}
\end{align}$

We now find $x$ and $y$ at the point $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{6}$.

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{x\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}\right)}&=2\sin\left(2\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}\right)\right)
\\\\
&=2\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)
\\\\
&=2\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)
\\\\
&=\maroonD{\sqrt{3}}
\\\\
\\\\
\purpleD{y\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}\right)}&=4\sin^2\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}\right)
\\\\
&=4\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2
\\\\
&=\purpleD{1}
\end{align}$

**Therefore the equation of our tangent line is: **

$y-\purpleD{1}=\greenD{\sqrt{3}}\left(x-\maroonD{\sqrt{3}}\right)$

The graph of the tangent is shown.

![A polar curve and a tangent line are graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 4 in increments of 1. The circumference units go from 0 to eleven sixths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one sixth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve is a circle with a radius of 2 centered on (2, one half pi). It passes through the following points. (2, five sixths pi), (3.5, two thirds pi), (3, one half pi), (3.5, one third pi), and (2, one sixth pi). A tangent line moves diagonally upward, intersecting the circle at (2, one sixth pi). All values estimated.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ba507f846b4fb53a1a065da88e99f156.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the polar curve $r=1+2\sin(\theta)$.

**What is the equation of the tangent line to the curve $r$ at $\theta = \dfrac{5\pi}{6}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The slope of the tangent line at a point is equal to $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at that point. In the case of polar curves, we can use the relationship:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\left( \dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right)}$

Then we can use the [[☃ definition 1]] to complete the equation for the tangent line through $\theta=\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$.

For a polar curve, $x=\goldE{r}\cos(\theta)$ and $y=\goldE{r}\sin(\theta)$. Therefore, in our problem we have:

$\begin{align}
x&=\goldE{(1+2\sin\theta)}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
y&=\goldE{(1+2\sin\theta)} \sin(\theta)
\end{align}$

We now find $x$ and $y$ at the point $\theta=\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$.

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{x\left(\blueD{\dfrac{5\pi}{6}}\right)}&=\left(1+2\sin\left(\blueD{\dfrac{5\pi}{6}}\right)\right)\cos\left(\blueD{\dfrac{5\pi}{6}}\right)
\\\\&=\left(1+2\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)\right)\left(-\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)
\\\\
&=\maroonD{-\sqrt{3}}
\\\\
\\\\
\purpleD{y\left(\blueD{\dfrac{5\pi}{6}}\right)}&=\left(1+2\sin\left(\blueD{\dfrac{5\pi}{6}}\right)\right) \sin\left(\blueD{\dfrac{5\pi}{6}}\right)
\\\\
&=\left(1+2\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)\right)\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)
\\\\
&=\purpleD{1}
\end{align}$

**Therefore the equation of our tangent line is:**

$\begin{align}
y-\purpleD{1}&=\greenD{-3\sqrt{3}}\left(x-(\maroonD{-\sqrt{3}})\right)
\\\\
y-1&=-3\sqrt{3}\left(x+\sqrt{3}\right)
\end{align}$

The graph of the tangent is shown.

![A polar curve and a tangent line are graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 3 in increments of 1. The circumference units go from 0 to eleven sixths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one sixth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve is a concentric loop that starts at the origin. The outer loop moves clockwise through points (1, pi), (2, five sixths pi), (3, one half pi), (2, one sixth pi), (1, 0), and (0, 0). The inner loop then moves through points (0.5, two thirds pi), (1, one half pi), (0.5, one third pi), and (0, 0). A tangent line moves diagonally downward, intersecting the curve at (2, five sixths pi). All values estimated.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d233d1e3d436ffde0c7d34265866419a.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the polar curve $r=\theta$.

**What is the equation of the tangent line to the curve $r$ at $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The slope of the tangent line at a point is equal to $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at that point. In the case of polar curves, we can use the relationship:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\left( \dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right)}$

Then we can use the [[☃ definition 1]] to complete the equation for the tangent line through $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$.

For a polar curve, $x=\goldE{r}\cos(\theta)$ and $y=\goldE{r}\sin(\theta)$. Therefore, in our problem we have:

$\begin{align}
x&=\goldE{\theta}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
y&=\goldE{\theta} \sin(\theta)
\end{align}$

We now find $x$ and $y$ at the point $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$.

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{x\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}\right)}&=\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}\right)\cos\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}\right)
\\\\
&=\dfrac{\pi}{2}(0)
\\\\
&=\maroonD{0}
\\\\
\\\\
\purpleD{y\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}\right)}&=\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}\right) \sin\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}\right)
\\\\
&=\dfrac{\pi}{2}(1)
\\\\
&=\purpleD{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}
\end{align}$

**Therefore the equation of our tangent line is:**

$\begin{align}
y-\purpleD{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}&=\greenD{-\dfrac{2}{\pi}}(x-\maroonD{0})
\\\\
y&=-\dfrac{2}{\pi}x+\dfrac{\pi}{2}
\end{align}$

The graph of the tangent is shown.

![A polar curve and a tangent line are graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 6 in increments of 1. The circumference units go from 0 to eleven sixths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one sixth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve moves upward right with increasing steepness from (0, 0) through (1, one third pi) to (1.5, one half pi), moves downward left through (2, two thirds pi) and (3, pi), then moves downward right through (4, four thirds pi), (4.5, three halves pi), and (5.5, eleven sixths pi). A tangent line moves diagonally downward, intersecting the curve at (1.5, one half pi). All values estimated.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4e99c040e3aab31c6255940fb7dd0c71.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the polar curve $r=2\cos(\theta)$ for $0\le \theta < \pi$.

**At which values of $\theta$ does the graph of $r$ have a horizontal tangent line?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A horizontal line has a slope of $0$. The slope of the tangent line at a point is equal to $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at that point. In the case of polar curves, we can use the relationship:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\left( \dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right)}$

Once we have an expression for the slope of the tangent line, we can look for the $\theta$-values that make $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}=0$ but don't make $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}=0$ (because then $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ will be undefined).

For a polar curve, $x=\goldE{r}\cos(\theta)$ and $y=\goldE{r}\sin(\theta)$. Therefore, in our problem we have:

$\begin{align}
x&=\goldE{2\cos(\theta)}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
&=2\cos^2(\theta)
\\\\
y&=\goldE{2\cos(\theta)} \sin(\theta)
\\\\
&=\sin(2\theta)
\end{align}$

Let's find $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}$ and $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}$. 

$\begin{align}
y(\theta)&=\sin(2\theta)
\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}&=2\cos(2\theta)
\\\\
\\\\
x(\theta)&=2\cos^2(\theta)
\\\\
\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}&=-2\cdot2\cos(\theta)\sin(\theta)
\\\\
&=-2\sin(2\theta)
\end{align}$

Now let's solve $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}=0$ on the interval $0\le \theta < \pi$.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}&=0
\\\\
2\cos(2\theta)&=0
\\\\
\cos(2\theta)&=0
\\\\
2\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{2}+k\cdot\pi&\text{, where }k\text{ is an integer}
\\\\
\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{4}+k\dfrac{\pi}{2}&\text{, where }k\text{ is an integer}
\end{align}$

Within our interval, our possibilities are $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ and $\theta=\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$.

Finally, we evaluate $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}$ for our two possible values of $\theta$ and require that $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}\ne0$.

$\begin{align}
\left.\dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right| _{\blueD{\theta =\tfrac{\pi }{4}}}&=-2\sin\left(2\cdot\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right)
\\\\
&=-2\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)
\\\\
&=-2
\\\\
\\\\
\left.\dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right| _{\greenD{\theta =\tfrac{3\pi }{4}}}&=-2\sin\left(2\cdot\greenD{\dfrac{3\pi}{4}}\right)
\\\\
&=-2\sin\left(\dfrac{3\pi}{2}\right)
\\\\
&=2
\end{align}$

**The graph of $r$ has a horizontal tangent at $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ or $\theta=\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$.**

The graphs of the tangents are shown.

![A polar curve and two tangent lines are graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 3 in increments of 1. The circumference units go from 0 to fifteen eighths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one eighth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve is a circle with a radius of 1 centered on (1, 0). It passes through the following points. (2, 0), (1, one fourth pi), (0, 0), and (1, seven fourths pi). A horizontal tangent line intersects the top of the circle at (1.5, one fourth pi). A second horizontal tangent line intersects the bottom of the circle at (1.5, seven fourths pi). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/13996390954b0d316144f05e7aaebdb944eb890d)

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the polar curve $r=2\tan(\theta)$ over the interval $\left( 0,2\pi \right)$.

**At which value of $\theta$ does the graph of $r$ have a vertical tangent line?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A vertical line has an undefined slope. The slope of the tangent line at a point is equal to $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at that point. In the case of polar curves, we can use the relationship:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\left( \dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right)}$

Once we have an expression for the slope of the tangent line, we can look for the $\theta$-values that make $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}=0$ but don't make $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}=0$ (because then $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ will be indeterminate).

For a polar curve, $x=\goldE{r}\cos(\theta)$ and $y=\goldE{r}\sin(\theta)$. Therefore, in our problem we have:

$\begin{align}
x&=\goldE{2\tan(\theta)}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
&=2\sin(\theta)
\\\\
y&=\goldE{2\tan(\theta)} \sin(\theta)
\end{align}$

Let's find $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}$ and $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}$. 

$\begin{align}
y(\theta)&=2\tan(\theta)\sin(\theta)
\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}&=2\sec^2(\theta)\sin(\theta)+2\tan(\theta)\cos(\theta)
\\\\
&=\dfrac{2\sin(\theta)}{\cos^2(\theta)}+2\sin(\theta)
\\\\
\\\\
x(\theta)&=2\sin(\theta)
\\\\
\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}&=2\cos(\theta)
\end{align}$

Now let's solve $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}=0$ on the interval $\left( 0,2\pi \right)$.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}&=0
\\\\
2\cos(\theta)&=0
\\\\
\cos(\theta)&=0
\end{align}$

Within our interval, our possible solutions are $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ or $\theta=\dfrac{3\pi}{2}$.
Notice, however, that $r(\theta)=2\tan(\theta)$ isn't defined at $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ or $\theta=\dfrac{3\pi}{2}$. If it's undefined at these points, it definitely doesn't have a tangent there.

**The graph of $r$ has no vertical tangents.**

![A polar curve is graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 6 in increments of 1. The circumference units go from 0 to eleven sixths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one sixth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve is symmetric about the origin, where the shape of the curve on each side of the origin is a parabola. The top curve is upward facing and the bottom is downward facing. The top curve moves downward through points (4, two thirds pi) and (1, five sixths pi) to (0, 0), then moves upward through (1, one sixth pi) and (4, one third pi). The bottom curve moves upward through (4, four thirds pi) and (1, seven sixths pi) to (0, 0), then moves downward through (1, eleven sixths pi) and (4, five thirds pi). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/49a8dbc50b5457bb7232de1fee45a9313671f115)

choices.content: The graph of $r$ has no vertical tangents.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the polar curve $r=e^\theta$ for $0\le \theta < 2\pi$.

**At which values of $\theta$ does the graph of $r$ have a horizontal tangent line?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A horizontal line has a slope of $0$. The slope of the tangent line at a point is equal to $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at that point. In the case of polar curves, we can use the relationship:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\left( \dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right)}$

Once we have an expression for the slope of the tangent line, we can look for the $\theta$-values that make $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}=0$ but don't make $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}=0$ (because then $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ will be undefined).

For a polar curve, $x=\goldE{r}\cos(\theta)$ and $y=\goldE{r}\sin(\theta)$. Therefore, in our problem we have:

$\begin{align}
x&=\goldE{e^\theta}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
y&=\goldE{e^\theta} \sin(\theta)
\end{align}$

Let's find $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}$ and $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}$.

$\begin{align}
y(\theta)&=e^\theta\sin(\theta)
\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}&=e^\theta\sin(\theta)+e^\theta\cos(\theta)
\\\\
&=e^\theta(\sin(\theta)+\cos(\theta))
\\\\
\\\\
x(\theta)&=e^\theta\cos(\theta)
\\\\
\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}&=e^\theta\cos(\theta)+e^\theta(-\sin(\theta))
\\\\
&=e^\theta(\cos(\theta)-\sin(\theta))
\end{align}$

Now let's solve $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}=0$ on the interval $0\le \theta < 2\pi$.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}&=0
\\\\
e^\theta(\sin(\theta)+\cos(\theta)) &=0
\\\\
\sin(\theta)+\cos(\theta)&=0
\\\\
\sin(\theta)&=-\cos(\theta)
\end{align}$

Within our interval, our possible solutions are $\theta=\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$ and $\theta=\dfrac{7\pi}{4}$.

Finally, we evaluate $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}$ for our two possible values of $\theta$ and require that $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}\ne0$.

$\begin{align}
\left.\dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right| _{\blueD{\theta =\tfrac{3\pi }{4}}}&=e^{^{\left(\blueD{\tfrac{3\pi}{4}}\right)}}\left(\cos\left(\blueD{\dfrac{3\pi}{4}}\right)-\sin\left(\blueD{\dfrac{3\pi}{4}}\right)\right)
\\\\
&=e^{^{\left({\tfrac{3\pi}{4}}\right)}}\left(-\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}-\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)
\\\\
&=-\sqrt{2}
e^{^{\left({\tfrac{3\pi}{4}}\right)}}
\\\\
\\\\
\left.\dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right| _{\greenD{\theta =\tfrac{7\pi }{4}}}&=e^{^{\left(\greenD{\tfrac{7\pi}{4}}\right)}}\left(\cos\left(\greenD{\dfrac{7\pi}{4}}\right)-\sin\left(\greenD{\dfrac{7\pi}{4}}\right)\right)
\\\\
&=e^{^{\left({\tfrac{7\pi}{4}}\right)}}\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}-\left(-\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)\right)
\\\\
&=\sqrt{2}e^{^{\left({\tfrac{7\pi}{4}}\right)}}
\end{align}$

**The graph of $r$ has a horizontal tangent at $\theta=\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$ or $\theta=\dfrac{7\pi}{4}$.**

The graphs of the tangents are shown with the figure at different scales.

![A polar curve and a tangent line are graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 15 in increments of 3. The circumference units go from 0 to fifteen eighths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one eighth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve moves upward right with increasing steepness from (1, 0) to (3, three eighths pi), then moves upward left through points (5, one half pi), (10, three fourth pi), and (12, seven eighths pi). A horizontal tangent line intersects the curve at (10, three fourths pi). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7e605cf581573edfd4956f73c01f1f16b72c47b0) ![A polar curve and a tangent line are graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 300 in increments of 100. The circumference units go from 0 to fifteen eighths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one eighth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve moves downward left with decreasing steepness from (0, 0) to (100, three halves pi), then continues downward right through (250, seven fourths pi) and (300, twenty nine sixteenths pi). A horizontal tangent line intersects the curve at (250, seven fourths pi). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/727abecd760d2d830ddf93ce4e427c6d73d2a304)

choices.content: The graph of $r$ has no horizontal tangents.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the polar curve $r=1+\cos(\theta)$ over the interval $\left( 0,\pi \right)$.

**At which value of $\theta$ does the graph of $r$ have a vertical tangent line?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A vertical line has an undefined slope. The slope of the tangent line at a point is equal to $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at that point. In the case of polar curves, we can use the relationship:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\left( \dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right)}$

Once we have an expression for the slope of the tangent line, we can look for the $\theta$-values that make $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}=0$ but don't make $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}=0$ (because then $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ will be indeterminate).

For a polar curve, $x=\goldE{r}\cos(\theta)$ and $y=\goldE{r}\sin(\theta)$. Therefore, in our problem we have:

$\begin{align}
x&=\goldE{(1+\cos(\theta))}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
&=\cos(\theta)+\cos^2(\theta)
\\\\
\\\\
y&=\goldE{(1+\cos(\theta))} \sin(\theta)
\\\\
&=\sin(\theta)+\cos(\theta)\sin(\theta)
\\\\
&=\sin(\theta)+\dfrac{1}{2}\sin(2\theta)
\end{align}$

Let's find $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}$ and $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}$.

$\begin{align}
y(\theta)&=\sin(\theta)+\dfrac{1}{2}\sin(2\theta)
\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}&=\cos(\theta)+\dfrac{1}{2}\cos(2\theta)(2)
\\\\
&=\cos(\theta)+\cos(2\theta)
\\\\
\\\\
x(\theta)&=\cos(\theta)+\cos^2(\theta)
\\\\
\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}&=-\sin(\theta)+2\cos(\theta)(-\sin(\theta)
\\\\
&=-\sin(\theta)-2\cos(\theta)\sin(\theta)
\end{align}$

Now let's solve $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}=0$ on the interval $\left( 0,\pi  \right)$.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}&=0
\\\\
-\sin(\theta)-2\cos(\theta)\sin(\theta)&=0
\\\\
-\sin(\theta)(1+2\cos(\theta))&=0
\\\\
\cos(\theta)=-\dfrac{1}{2}&\text{ or }\sin(\theta)=0
\end{align}$

Within our interval, our possible solution is $\theta=\dfrac{2\pi}{3}$.

Finally, we evaluate $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}$ for our possible value of $\theta$ and require that $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}\ne0$.

$\begin{align}
\left.\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right| _{\blueD{\theta =\tfrac{2\pi }{3}}}&=\cos\left(\blueD{\dfrac{2\pi}{3}}\right)+\cos\left(2\left(\blueD{\dfrac{2\pi}{3}}\right)\right)
\\\\
&=\cos\left({\dfrac{2\pi}{3}}\right)+\cos\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{3}\right)
\\\\
&=-\dfrac{1}{2}-\dfrac{1}{2}
\\\\
&=-1
\end{align}$

**The graph of $r$ has a vertical tangent at $\theta=\dfrac{2\pi}{3}$.**

The graph of the tangent is shown.

![A polar curve and a tangent line are graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 2 in increments of 1. The circumference units go from 0 to eleven sixths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one sixth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve moves upward left with increasing steepness from (0, 0) through (0.5, two thirds pi) to (1, one half pi), then moves downward right through (1.5, one third pi) and (2, 0). A vertical tangent line intersects the curve at (0.5, two thirds pi). All values estimated.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cdcfb8095c4ec9ecc1f9aab9bd5a7a72.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the polar curve $r=1-\sin(\theta)$ for $0\le \theta < 2\pi$.

**At which values of $\theta$ does the graph of $r$ have a horizontal tangent line?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A horizontal line has a slope of $0$. The slope of the tangent line at a point is equal to $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at that point. In the case of polar curves, we can use the relationship:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\left( \dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right)}$

Once we have an expression for the slope of the tangent line, we can look for the $\theta$-values that make $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}=0$ but don't make $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}=0$ (because then $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ will be undefined).

For a polar curve, $x=\goldE{r}\cos(\theta)$ and $y=\goldE{r}\sin(\theta)$. Therefore, in our problem we have:

$\begin{align}
x&=\goldE{(1-\sin(\theta))}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
&=\cos(\theta)-\sin(\theta)\cos(\theta)
\\\\
&=\cos(\theta)-\dfrac{1}{2}\sin(2\theta)
\\\\
\\\\
y&=\goldE{(1-\sin(\theta))} \sin(\theta)
\\\\
&=\sin(\theta)-\sin^2(\theta)
\end{align}$

Let's find $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}$ and $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}$.

$\begin{align}
y(\theta)&=\sin(\theta)-\sin^2(\theta)
\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}&=\cos(\theta)-2\sin(\theta)\cos(\theta)
\\\\
&=\cos(\theta)-2\sin(\theta)\cos(\theta)
\\\\
\\\\
x(\theta)&=\cos(\theta)-\dfrac{1}{2}\sin(2\theta)
\\\\
\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}&=-\sin(\theta)-\dfrac{1}{2}\cos(2\theta)(2)
\\\\
&=-\sin(\theta)-\cos(2\theta)
\end{align}$

Now let's solve $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}=0$ on the interval $0\le \theta < 2\pi$.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}&=0
\\\\
\cos(\theta)-2\sin(\theta)\cos(\theta)&=0
\\\\
\cos(\theta)(1-2\sin(\theta))&=0
\\\\
\cos(\theta)=0&\text{ or }\sin(\theta)=\dfrac{1}{2}
\end{align}$

Within our interval, our possible solutions are $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$, $\theta=\dfrac{3\pi}{2}$, $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{6}$, or $\theta=\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$ .

Finally, we evaluate $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}$ for our four possible values of $\theta$ and require that $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}\ne0$.

$\begin{align}
\left.\dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right| _{\blueD{\theta =\tfrac{\pi }{2}}}&=-\sin\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}\right)-\cos\left(2\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}\right)\right)
\\\\
&=-(1)-\cos(\pi)
\\\\
&=-1-(-1)
\\\\
&=0
\\\\
\\\\
\left.\dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right| _{\greenD{\theta =\tfrac{3\pi }{2}}}&=-\sin\left(\greenD{\dfrac{3\pi}{2}}\right)-\cos\left(2\left(\greenD{\dfrac{3\pi}{2}}\right)\right)
\\\\
&=-(-1)-\cos(3\pi)
\\\\
&=1-(-1)
\\\\
&=2
\\\\
\\\\
\left.\dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right| _{\purpleD{\theta =\tfrac{\pi }{6}}}&=-\sin\left(\purpleD{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}\right)-\cos\left(2\left(\purpleD{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}\right)\right)
\\\\
&=-\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)-\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)
\\\\
&=-\dfrac{1}{2}-\dfrac{1}{2}
\\\\
&=-1
\\\\
\\\\
\left.\dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right| _{\maroonD{\theta =\tfrac{5\pi }{6}}}&=-\sin\left(\maroonD{\dfrac{5\pi}{6}}\right)-\cos\left(2\left(\maroonD{\dfrac{5\pi}{6}}\right)\right)
\\\\
&=-\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)-\cos\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{3}\right)
\\\\
&=-\dfrac{1}{2}-\dfrac{1}{2}
\\\\
&=-1
\end{align}$

**The graph of $r$ has a horizontal tangent at $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{6}$ or $\theta=\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$ or $\theta=\dfrac{3\pi}{2}$. **


**Note**: It turns out that the curve is not differentiable at $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$, where the graph comes to a "point," as seen below. 

The graphs of the tangents are shown.

![A polar curve and two tangent lines are graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 2.5 in increments of 0.5. The circumference units go from 0 to eleven sixths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one sixth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve is a downward facing cardioid curve which is roughly heart shaped with a semicircular bottom. It starts at the origin and moves counterclockwise through the following points. (0.5, five sixths pi), (1, pi), (1.5, seven sixths pi), (2, three halves pi), (1.5, eleven sixths pi), (1, 0), (0.5, one sixth pi), and (0, 0). A horizontal tangent line intersects the top of the curve at (0.5, one sixth pi) and (0.5, five sixths pi). A second horizontal tangent line intersects the bottom of the curve at (2, three halves pi). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/650f77578fcafcd846dc1852af2ee6c9b076d72f)

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the polar curve $r=e^{\theta+1}$ over the interval $\left( 0,2\pi \right)$.

**At which value of $\theta$ does the graph of $r$ have a vertical tangent line?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A vertical line has an undefined slope. The slope of the tangent line at a point is equal to $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at that point. In the case of polar curves, we can use the relationship:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\left( \dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \right)}$

Once we have an expression for the slope of the tangent line, we can look for the $\theta$-values that make $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}=0$ but don't make $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}=0$ (because then $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ will be indeterminate).

For a polar curve, $x=\goldE{r}\cos(\theta)$ and $y=\goldE{r}\sin(\theta)$. Therefore, in our problem we have:

$\begin{align}
x&=\goldE{e^{\theta+1}}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
y&=\goldE{e^{\theta+1}} \sin(\theta)
\end{align}$

Let's find $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}$ and $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}$.

$\begin{align}
y(\theta)&=e^{\theta+1}\sin(\theta)
\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{d\theta}&=e^{\theta+1}(1)\sin(\theta)+e^{\theta+1}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
&=e^{\theta+1}(\sin(\theta)+\cos(\theta))
\\\\
\\\\
x(\theta)&=e^{\theta+1}\cos(\theta)
\\\\
\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}&=e^{\theta+1}(1)\cos(\theta)+e^{\theta+1}(-\sin(\theta))
\\\\
&=e^{\theta+1}(\cos(\theta)-\sin(\theta))
\end{align}$

Now let's solve $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}=0$ on the interval $\left( 0,2\pi  \right)$.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}&=0
\\\\
e^{\theta+1}(\cos(\theta)-\sin(\theta))&=0
\\\\
\cos(\theta)-\sin(\theta)&=0
\\\\
\cos(\theta)&=\sin(\theta)
\end{align}$

Within our interval, our possible solutions are $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ and $\theta=\dfrac{5\pi}{4}$.

Finally, we evaluate $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}$ for our two possible values of $\theta$ and require that $\dfrac{dx}{d\theta}\ne0$.

$\begin{align}
\left.\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right| _{\blueD{\theta =\footnotesize\dfrac{\pi }{4}}}&=e^{^{\left(\blueD{\footnotesize\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right)\normalsize +1}}\cdot\left(\sin\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right)+\cos\left(\blueD{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right)\right)
\\\\
&=e^{^{\left({\footnotesize\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right)\normalsize +1}}\cdot\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}+\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)
\\\\
&=\sqrt{2}e^{^{\left({\footnotesize\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right)\normalsize +1}}
\\\\
\\\\
\left.\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \right| _{\greenD{\theta =\footnotesize\dfrac{5\pi }{4}}}&=e^{^{\left(\greenD{\footnotesize\dfrac{5\pi}{4}}\normalsize +1\right)}}\cdot\left(\sin\left(\greenD{\dfrac{5\pi}{4}}\right)+\cos\left(\greenD{\dfrac{5\pi}{4}}\right)\right)
\\\\
&=e^{^{\left({\footnotesize\dfrac{5\pi}{4}}\normalsize +1\right)}}\cdot\left(-\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}+\left(-\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)\right)
\\\\
&=-\sqrt{2}e^{^{\left({\footnotesize\dfrac{5\pi}{4}}\normalsize +1\right)}}
\end{align}$

**The graph of $r$ has vertical tangents at $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ or $\theta=\dfrac{5\pi}{4}$.**

The graphs of the tangents are shown with the figure at different scales.

![A polar curve and a tangent line are graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 50 in increments of 10. The circumference units go from 0 to fifteen eighths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one eighth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve moves upward right with increasing steepness from (0, 0) through (5, one fourth pi) to (15, one half pi), then continues left through (20, five eighths pi) and (40, seven eighths pi). A vertical tangent line intersects the curve at (5, one fourth pi). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cde6389e47f2cb673aa59e0129a92a04750ecacd) ![A polar curve and a tangent line are graphed in a polar coordinate plane. The radius units go from 0 to 600 in increments of 200. The circumference units go from 0 to fifteen eighths pi, then back to 0, in increments of one eighth pi, starting center right and moving counterclockwise. The curve moves downward left with increasing steepness from (0, 0) through (100, five fourths pi) to (200, eleven eighths pi), then continues right through (300, three halves pi) and (450, thirteen eighths pi). A vertical tangent line intersects the curve at (100, five fourths pi). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8aa614113d6d1a122f890947618674b581533336)
